title: McInerney [Minor] -v- Kildare County Council
text:
THE HIGH COURT
[2026] IEHC 61
[Record No. 2017/2163P]
BETWEEN
PATRICK MCINERNEY (A MINOR) SUING BY AND THROUGH HIS
MOTHER AND NEXT FRIEND, HELEN MCINERNEY
PLAINTIFF
AND
KILDARE COUNTY COUNCIL
DEFENDANT
JUDGMENT of Mr Justice Barr delivered on 13th day of February 2026.
Introduction.
1. There are two applications before the court. The first is a motion brought by the
plaintiff by notice of motion dated 22 May 2025 wherein the plaintiff seeks a direction
that there should be a modular trial, whereby the issue of liability should be determined
by the trial judge first; to be followed by a hearing on quantum, in the event that the
trial judge finds some liability in favour of the plaintiff. Secondly, the plaintiff seeks an

2
order that if a modular trial is directed by this Court, the issue as to whether quantum
should be decided on an interim basis, or on a whole life basis, should be left over to
the decision of the trial judge.
2. The second application before the court is the defendant’s motion dated 25
November 2024, wherein it seeks an order directing the plaintiff to furnish full
particulars of his claim for special damages on a whole life basis.
3. In order to understand the basis of the application made on behalf of the plaintiff
for a modular trial, it is necessary to set out in some detail the background to these
proceedings and the issues that arise in relation thereto.
Background to the Proceedings.
4. The plaintiff is a young man, who was born on 6 April 2010. At the time of the
accident, he was living with his parents at a halting site known as Ardrew Halting Site,
Athy, Co Kildare.
5. It is the plaintiff's case that on 15 June 2015, when he was five years of age, he
ascended onto the top of a wall surrounding the halting site. This wall was
approximately 9 feet (3m) in height. It is alleged that the plaintiff was able to get onto
the top of the wall due to the fact that mounds of earth, rubbish and other debris had
been left leaning against the wall, thereby permitting easy access onto the top of the
wall.
6. It is the plaintiff's case that this mound of earth, rubbish and debris had been
present for quite some time. The plaintiff pleads that children at the halting site had
been using the mound of earth to gain access onto the top of the wall. In addition, it is
pleaded that the top of the wall itself was in a dangerous and defective condition due to

3
the fact that the capping stones thereon had broken away meaning that the top of the
wall was unsafe and dangerous.
7. The exact circumstances of the accident are pleaded in the following way at
para. 3 in the personal injury summons:
“On or about the 15th day of June 2015 the infant plaintiff was playing in an
open area on the halting site and proceeded to climb a wall, during the course
of which, the infant plaintiff fell and suffered serious personal injuries, loss,
damages, inconvenience and expense.
The defendant had caused, allowed and/or permitted a mound of earth and other
debris to be banked up against the wall. This mound of earth constituted a trap
for children and would invariably attract children. By reason of the presence of
the mound of earth together with the debris, the infant plaintiff was able to climb
up on the wall.
The defendant, its servants or agents failed to remove the mound of earth and
other debris, failed to properly secure their property preventing access to
children to the area.”
8. It is common case that as a result of falling from the wall the infant plaintiff
suffered a fracture of his skull. This was demonstrated on a CT scan taken on the night
of the accident.
9. Proceedings were brought on behalf of the infant plaintiff by personal injury
summons issued on 8 March 2017. After the exchange of a notice for particulars and
replies thereto, a defence was filed on behalf of the defendant on 8 October 2019. This
was a fairly standard form defence, which put liability and quantum in issue. It also
included a plea that damage had been caused to a fence adjacent to the wall, which

4
damage had been caused by the unlawful acts of third parties unknown to the
defendant. The defence also pleaded that if the accident occurred in the manner alleged
by the plaintiff, or at all, the injuries sustained by the plaintiff were contributed to by
the negligence and breach of duty on the part of his parents for failure to adequately
supervise him while playing in the halting site. The defence set out particulars of the
negligence and breach of duty that was alleged in this regard. Essentially the defence
put all matters in issue.
10. On consent of the parties, the defendant was given liberty to amend its
defence. It delivered an amended defence on 19 May 2025. There were three significant
amendments made to the defence that had been filed on its behalf. Firstly, an additional
plea was added to the defence to the effect that the plaintiff's claim to special damages
had been exaggerated. Secondly, it was pleaded that the defendant had obtained
information from a member of An Garda Síochána to the effect that while the plaintiff's
mother and next friend was incarcerated in prison, recordings had been made of her
telephone conversations with her husband. It was alleged that in two telephone calls
made on 19 April 2024 and 21 April 2024, the plaintiff's father had informed the
plaintiff’s mother that he had paid the sum of €2,000 in two instalments to a witness to
secure his attendance to give evidence on behalf of the plaintiff at the trial of the
action. The defence pleaded that this witness appeared as a witness of fact on the
plaintiff's schedule of witnesses. It was pleaded that a case file had been prepared by
An Garda Síochána and had been forwarded to the Director of Public Prosecutions in
relation to the possible commission of criminal offences in relation to the matters that
had been disclosed in these telephone conversations. The defence went on to plead that
the payment of the sums of money to the witness to secure his attendance at the trial of

5
the action was evidence of collusion between the plaintiff's father and mother and the
witness.
11. Finally, a further amendment was made to the defence which pleaded that the
plaintiff had not suffered the skull fracture in the manner alleged by him in his
pleadings, but had done so by reason of his interaction with a horse or pony at a time
and place unknown to the defendant.
12. It is necessary to go into the detail of the injuries that are pleaded as having been
suffered by the plaintiff in some detail. At the outset, the case was pleaded on the basis
that the plaintiff had suffered a significant skull fracture, together with an injury leading
to loss of hearing in the right ear. It was also pleaded that subsequent to the accident,
his pre-existing documented behavioural issues, had been severely exacerbated by the
injuries sustained in the accident.
13. The injuries pleaded by the plaintiff took a fairly dramatic turn after 24
September 2023. That was the date on which Dr. Declan O'Rourke, Consultant
Paediatric Neurologist, had provided a report to the plaintiff's solicitor in relation to the
injuries sustained by the plaintiff in the accident. This report provided expert evidence
to the effect that the plaintiff had suffered a severe traumatic brain injury following the
fall from a significant height. Dr. O'Rourke was of the opinion that the brain injury was
severe in its manifestation. He stated that the plaintiff would require care or support
both during the day and at night for the remainder of his life, for his own safety and for
the safety of others. Dr. O'Rourke was further of the view that the plaintiff's degree of
behavioural difficulties, inattention and overall cognitive functioning, would mean that
he will not attain the ability to live independently.
14. Dr O'Rourke was also of opinion that the plaintiff would never achieve
meaningful employment. He noted that the plaintiff had profound hearing loss in his

6
right ear. He was of opinion that that was directly due to his severe traumatic head
injury. He also noted that the plaintiff’s severe ADHD features were significantly
impacting on his overall daily functioning.
15. On 10 January 2026, a report was furnished by Dr Mankad, who is a paediatric
neurologist practising at Great Ormond Street Hospital for Children, London. He has
been a consultant in that hospital since 2011. He has headed the neuroradiology services
at that hospital since 2018. He was appointed to the post of Director of Radiology in
2025. He holds an honorary associate professorship with University College London
and University College Dublin. The essence of his opinion as contained in that report
is that having reviewed the CT scans that were taken on the night of the accident, he is
satisfied that there was evidence thereon of brain injury.
16. The defendant’s position is diametrically opposed to that of the plaintiff. As
noted from the pleadings, the defendant disputes all aspects of the accident. It denies
that the accident occurred in the manner alleged by the plaintiff or at all. In particular,
it denies that the plaintiff fell from the wall in question in the manner alleged by him. It
alleges that the skull fracture that was disclosed on the CT scan was suffered by him
due to his interaction with a horse or pony, presumably by either falling off the animal
or by being kicked in the head by it.
17. As has been set out in the replying affidavit sworn by the defendant’s solicitor,
Mr. David Andrews on 28 November 2025, all aspects of the claim in relation to
quantum is in dispute between the parties. In particular, the defendant's medical experts
dispute the assertion that the plaintiff suffered any brain injury as a result of the fracture
to his skull. In addition, it is alleged that any behavioural or cognitive difficulties
suffered by the plaintiff since the time of the accident are due to his pre-existing ADHD
condition, which was documented prior to the date of the accident, and are due to the

7
somewhat chaotic living circumstances in which the plaintiff has been brought
up. These are set out in very considerable detail by Mr. Andrews at para. 21 et seq of
his affidavit. It is further alleged by the defendant that the medical records that have
been made available concerning the plaintiff show a persistent lack of engagement by
the plaintiff and his parents with various modalities of treatment that had been made
available to him and in particular a failure to engage with the CAMHS supports that
had been made available.
18. Mr. Andrews has forcefully argued that the plaintiff and his next friend and his
father have deliberately avoided utilising the treatment and supports that have been
offered to him and have misled their own medical experts with a view to exaggerating
the plaintiff’s claim to damages in these proceedings. This has been pleaded in the
following way at para. 64 of his affidavit:
“I say that there is and has been a deliberate attempt by the plaintiff's parents
to exaggerate the nature and extent of the plaintiff's personal injuries and
alleged resulting sequalae. I believe that they have been acting intentionally in
attempting to mislead the plaintiff's experts and the courts.”
19. The foregoing is but a very brief summary of the extent of the matters that are
in issue in the proceedings. It is fair to say that in this action absolutely everything is in
issue between the parties. There is an allegation that the accident did not occur at all as
described by the plaintiff; there is an allegation that the plaintiff's parents have colluded
with a third party, presumably with the intention of providing false evidence at the trial
of the action; there is a denial of any negligence or breach of duty on the part of the
defendant its servants or agents; there is an allegation that the accident was caused or
contributed to by the negligence of the plaintiff’s parents in failing to supervise him
adequately or at all on the day of the accident; with the exception of the skull fracture

8
almost every other aspect of the injuries pleaded by the plaintiff are strenuously
disputed by the defendant both in relation to their severity and in relation to their being
caused by the accident the subject matter of the proceedings.
20. Given the level of injury and disability pleaded by the plaintiff, in particular that
he has suffered a serious brain injury as a result of the accident which has left him in
need of care on a 24-hour basis; which has rendered him incapable of independent
living; and which has rendered him incapable of gainful employment; the capital value
of this claim at full value is very considerable. On one view of the figures, it could reach
an award of damages greater than €25m. It is against that background that the current
application for a modular trial has to be considered.
Submissions on behalf of the Plaintiff.
21. On behalf of the plaintiff, Mr. Tracey SC submitted that having regard to the
very extensive liability issues that arise on the pleadings, whereby literally all matters
are in issue, ranging from the allegation that the accident did not occur at all, in that the
plaintiff did not fall from a wall, but was kicked or fell from a horse or pony, to the
denial that the defendant was guilty of the alleged or any negligence or breach of duty,
coupled with the plea that the accident was caused or contributed to by the negligence
of the plaintiff's parents, will mean that the liability aspect of the case will be of
significant duration.
22. Counsel stated that as of the plaintiff's most recent schedule of witnesses who
would be necessary to give evidence at the trial of the action, there were no less than
sixteen expert witnesses and five witnesses as to fact listed thereon. It was submitted
that the vast majority of the witnesses set out in the schedule were medical experts,
rehabilitation experts and financial experts, who would be necessary to give evidence

9
in relation to the quantum aspects of the case. It was submitted that the only witnesses
that would be necessary on the liability aspects were the five witnesses as to fact, being
the plaintiff, his mother, his father, Mr. McC, who had found the plaintiff on the ground
at the foot of the wall and who had helped him up and brought him home, and Mr. O
(the court was not informed what evidence he might give); together with the plaintiff's
engineer as an expert witness.
23. It was submitted that having regard to the very extensive liability issues in the
case, it was entirely reasonable that the liability aspects should be determined first by
the trial judge. If he or she were to find that the plaintiff did not fall from the wall at the
halting site as alleged, but suffered his injury either by interaction with a horse or in
some other manner, that would be the end of the case. The plaintiff's action against the
defendant would be dismissed. There would be no need to embark upon the complex
issues that arose in relation to the existence of any brain injury, whether it was caused
by the fall from the wall, and whether the resulting behavioural and cognitive
difficulties suffered by the plaintiff were due to the brain injury or were in fact just a
manifestation of his pre-existing ADHD difficulties, which were not exacerbated by the
accident.
24. Counsel further submitted that the court should have regard to the fact that these
were very substantial proceedings that were being brought on behalf of an infant
plaintiff who had severe learning and behavioural difficulties, whether or not they were
caused by the accident. The court was urged to have regard to the fact that he was a
person who came from a severely deprived background. He was a member of the
travelling community. Both his parents had spent time in prison as a result of criminal
convictions. These difficulties had been set out in some detail by the defendant’s
solicitor in his replying affidavit. It was submitted that in these circumstances the court

10
should have regard to the fact that the plaintiff would experience enormous difficulty
in obtaining the services of the expert witnesses from whom reports had been
commissioned, at the trial of the action. It was submitted that if the issue of liability
was determined as a stand-alone issue first and if that was found in favour of the
plaintiff, that would enable him to obtain funds on the basis of the award that he would
ultimately get with which he could secure the attendance of the expert witnesses
identified on the updated schedule of witnesses dated 14 January 2026.
25. It was submitted that the interests of justice and the saving of court time were
in favour of directing a modular trial, due to the fact that once the complex issues on
liability were determined, then the court could proceed to deal with the quantum issue
either on a whole life basis or on an interim basis, as it thought fit.
26. It was submitted that there was no prejudice to the defendant in hiving off the
issue of liability, which would include the question of whether or not the accident
happened by falling off a wall at all, from the complex issues of causation in relation to
the injuries, which would centre on three key aspects being (a) whether the plaintiff
suffered a brain injury on 15 June 2015, and, if so, (b) whether his ongoing behavioural
and cognitive difficulties were due to that brain injury, or were merely a manifestation
of his pre-existing ADHD, and, if so, (c) to what extent that condition had been
exacerbated by the injuries sustained in the accident.
27. It was submitted that the determination of the court on those issues of causation
of the physical injuries and the extent thereof, would dictate what amount of special
damages would be properly recoverable at the end of the day.

11
Submissions on behalf of the Defendant.
28. On behalf of the defendant, Mr. McCarthy SC submitted that the present case
raised questions of fraud, collusion and exaggeration which permeated both the issues
of liability and quantum. It was submitted that the credibility of the plaintiff's mother
and next friend and of his father and of the alleged independent witness, Mr. McC,
would be central to the determination of liability, and if necessary, to the issue of
quantum.
29. It was submitted that when the credibility of the infant’s parents was in issue, it
was essential that the defendant be allowed to cross-examine freely and fully across the
entire range of credibility issues in the case. This would involve a detailed examination
of what the plaintiff's mother and father had told various medical and other experts,
both for the plaintiff and for the defendant, who had been retained to provide reports in
the case.
30. Counsel submitted that there were a number of serious credibility issues that
arose in connection with the quantum aspects of the case. By way of example, he stated
that in September 2017, when the plaintiff was then aged seven years, he was brought
to a GP, Dr Donal McCarthy, for the purpose of that doctor examining him and
providing a certificate that he was in a fit condition to participate in the sport of
boxing. To that end, Dr McCarthy's clinical note reported that the plaintiff was "well
apart from ADHD". The doctor signed a certificate for the Irish Amateur Boxing
Association on 15 September 2017, in which it essentially recorded that the plaintiff
had no ongoing health issues other than the pre-existing condition of ADHD. In
particular, under the heading any history of fracture(s), it was recorded as "nil". This
certificate was dated and signed by the GP.

12
31. Counsel stated that the defendant had been furnished with a copy of the medical
notes made by the GP and with a copy of the certificate that he had signed on that
occasion. The defendant was currently carrying out investigations to see if, having
obtained that certificate, the plaintiff participated in the sport of boxing; and if so, to
what extent he participated in it in the years between 2017 and 2023, when he was
examined by Dr Declan O’Rourke.
32. Counsel pointed out that at section 3 of his medical report dated 24 September
2023, Dr O'Rourke had set out an extensive account of the plaintiff's premorbid
personality and difficulties and had given an equally extensive history of the plaintiff's
difficulties between the date of the accident and the date of that examination in
September 2023. At para. 3.18 the doctor had noted as follows: "He is unable to cycle
his bicycle. He has interest in boxing but is not allowed to box given his head injury.”
Thus, it would appear that Dr O'Rourke was told that the plaintiff was interested in
boxing, but was not able to participate in that sport due to his head injury. It does not
appear that Dr O'Rourke was told that in 2017, the plaintiff had actually attended with
a GP and had obtained a certificate that would have entitled him to participate in the
sport of boxing. Whether he did so, remains to be determined.
33. Counsel stated that the medical records disclosed that in January 2018, the
plaintiff's mother had told a consultant psychiatrist in Crumlin Children's Hospital that
the plaintiff had been in a coma for six days after the accident in June 2015. That would
not appear to be the case, as there is no mention of the plaintiff ever being in a coma in
the medical records that have been disclosed to date.
34. Counsel submitted that there was the evidence of Garda Cullinane in relation to
the two phone calls that had taken place while the plaintiff's mother and next friend was
in the women's prison in Mountjoy, in which the plaintiff's father was recorded as

13
having told the plaintiff's mother that he had made two substantial payments to a
witness, to ensure his attendance at the trial of the action.
35. Counsel stated that the defendant had two reports from a nursing care
consultant, Ms Perryman, wherein she had noted that the plaintiff had exhibited certain
behaviour when she met him in the company of his father; but that these behavioural
traits were entirely absent when she had met the plaintiff subsequently in the company
of his mother; suggesting that the plaintiff might have been following instructions as to
what behaviour to exhibit at the time of the first examination.
36. Counsel submitted that the claim for special damages made by the plaintiff in
these proceedings, had risen from a modest initial claim for travelling expenses of €900,
to a claim for €1500 when the action had been set down for hearing by the plaintiff;
which had risen further to an ultimate claim for slightly over €25m as set out in the
actuary’s report furnished on behalf of the plaintiff. It was submitted that the defendant
should be entitled to cross-examine on the legitimacy of that enormous special damages
claim before the trial judge reached a conclusion on the credibility of the plaintiff's
claim in relation to the liability aspects.
37. Counsel stated that it was also relevant by way of background that each of the
plaintiff's parents had been convicted and sentenced to terms of imprisonment for
crimes of dishonesty; namely offences connected to money laundering. It was
submitted that the defendant was entitled to explore the criminal history of the plaintiff's
parents as part of its cross examination of them.
38. It was submitted that if the defendant was to properly test the credit of the
plaintiff and his witnesses on liability, the defendant would have to call several of its
own medical witnesses to establish what statements were made by the plaintiff’s parents
to them when giving a history of his behavioural issues both pre and post-accident. It

14
was submitted that it would be entirely wasteful to have to call these witnesses twice;
once to give evidence as to what they were told by the plaintiff's parents at various
medical examinations and again in a subsequent module on quantum in relation to his
injuries and prognosis for the future.
39. In addition, it was submitted that it would be essential for the defendant to be
able to question Dr O'Rourke as to whether he was informed of (a) the examination
carried out by the GP in 2017 for the purpose of providing the certificate for the
Amateur Boxing Association; and (b) that he had been told by the plaintiff's next friend
that while the plaintiff had an interest in boxing, he had not been able to participate in
that sport due to a previous head injury. While it was accepted that the defendant did
not have any evidence that the plaintiff had engaged in boxing, its investigations in this
regard were continuing.
40. It was submitted that if it turned out to be the case that Dr O'Rourke had been
told that the plaintiff had not engaged in boxing, and if he had in fact engaged in that
sport, that would be highly relevant both as to what information was given to the doctor
and its effect on his subsequent opinion as to the causation of the sequelae of the brain
injury as given in his report. It was submitted that it was essential that the defendant be
given the opportunity to fully examine this aspect of the case by way of cross-
examination at the trial of the action.
41. Counsel pointed out that a claim for accommodation had been put forward in
the sum of approximately €1.4 million, but the reality was that the plaintiff’s parents
were on the housing list maintained by the defendant. When requested to provide details
of what enquiries or actions they had taken to secure housing from the defendant, they
had refused to state what steps they had taken in this regard.

15
42. In summary, counsel stated that the defendant resisted this application for a
modular trial on the basis that if that was done, its forensic examination of the credibility
of the plaintiff and his witnesses would be severely prejudiced.
43. Even if the defendant was allowed to roam far and wide in its cross examination
of the plaintiff's parents and other witnesses, it would have to call a number of its own
witnesses to prove what the plaintiff’s mother or father had told them in the course of
various medical examinations. It was submitted that this would lead to enormous waste
of both court time and costs if it were necessary for these witnesses to be called twice,
once on the credibility issue as part of the liability module and a second time in the
course of the quantum module.
44. It was submitted that in all the circumstances of this case, the court should direct
that the trial of the action should proceed in the usual way as a unitary trial.
The Law.
45. The test which should be applied when a court is asked to consider directing a
modular trial has been set down in a number of well-known cases: see Cork Plastics
(Manufacturing) v Ineos Compound UK Limited [2008] IEHC 93; McCann v Desmond
[2010] 4 IR 554; Weavering Macro Fixed Income Fund Limited (In Liquidation) v PNC
Global and Ors. [2012] IESC 60; and IT (A Minor) v HSE [2025] IECA 291.
46. It is not necessary to review these cases in extenso. The statement of principles
that was given by Charleton J in the McCann case has been referred to with approval in
a number of subsequent cases. The court is satisfied that this represents a correct
statement of the principles that have to be applied by this Court when determining this
application.

16
47. In the McCann case Charleton J set out these principles at para.7 of his judgment
as follows:
“Therefore, given that the default position is a full hearing, I believe that the
questions which would naturally address themselves to the mind of a court in
considering an application such as this for a modular hearing, would include:-
(1) Are the issues to be tried by way of a preliminary module, readily
capable of determination in isolation from the other issues in dispute
between the parties? A modular order should not be made if the case
could be characterised as an organic whole, the taking out from which
of a series of issues would tear the fabric of what the parties need to
litigate, so that the case of either of the plaintiff or the defendant would
be damaged through being seen in the isolated context of a hearing on
a number of limited issues.
(2) Has a clear saving in the time of the court and the costs that the
parties might have to bear been identified? The court should not readily
embark on a modular hearing simply because of a contention that a
saving in time and costs has been identified, but rather it should view
that factor in the context of the need to administer justice in the entire
circumstances of the case.
(3) Would a modular order result in any prejudice to the parties? If, for
instance, the issue as to what damage was occasioned by reason of the
wrong alleged by the plaintiff was so intricately woven in to the proofs
that were necessary to the proof of liability for the wrong, so that the

17
removal of the issue of damages would undermine the strength of the
plaintiff's case, or the response which a defendant might make to it, then
the order should not be made.(4) Is a motion a device to suit the moving
party or does it genuinely assist the litigation by being of help to the
resolution of the issues? I return to the idea that a judge should always
be aware that tactical decisions are made, often out of an abundance of
enthusiasm, by parties to litigation, who may seek to put the other party
at a disadvantage through the obtaining of an order under the Rules of
the Superior Courts 1986, or one capable of being made within the
inherent jurisdiction of the court. Obvious examples of pre-trial motions
that may merely be tactical are motions to strike out proceedings as
being vexatious or frivolous or to seek an order for security for costs
under s. 390 of the Companies Act 1963. Other instances include the
lengthy arguments that can sometimes ensue in relation to discovery. If
the removal of issues to a modular hearing is likely to disadvantage the
proper process of pre-trial preparation that discovery orders, notices
for particulars and notices to admit facts involve, then such a motion
should be refused as resulting not from a genuine process that will assist
the trial but for tactical reasons related to wrong footing the other
party.”

18
Discussion and Conclusions.
48. This case has a number of complexities. There are allegations of fraud, collusion
and exaggeration. Leaving aside the hotly contested issue of liability, almost every
aspect of the claim for damages is in issue.
49. With the exception of the existence of a skull fracture, almost every other aspect
of the injuries alleged to have been suffered by the plaintiff, is contested. It is denied
that the plaintiff suffered any brain injury; the extent of that injury is contested. It is
denied that the plaintiff's hearing loss is referable to the accident. It is denied that the
plaintiff's behavioural and cognitive difficulties are due to the accident. It is alleged that
these difficulties stem from his pre-existing ADHD diagnosis and/or are due to his
social and family circumstances.
50. There is an allegation that the plaintiff and his parents have engaged in
deliberate exaggeration of his symptoms both in the accounts given to their own doctors
and in the accounts given to the medical experts retained on behalf of the defendant. It
is alleged that this was done with a view to wrongly obtaining a very large award of
damages on behalf of the plaintiff.
51. It is alleged that the consequential claim for special damages is also tainted by
fraud and exaggeration.
52. It is fair to say that every conceivable aspect of the plaintiff's claim is
contested. The only matters that do not appear to be contested are (a) that the plaintiff
resided at the halting site; (b) that he was five years of age in June 2015; and (c) that he
suffered a skull fracture as demonstrated on the CT scan taken on the evening of 15
June 2015.
53. In these circumstances, it would appear that the following are the issues that
will have to be determined at the trial of the action: on the liability aspect of the case

19
the court will have to determine (i) did the plaintiff sustain his skull fracture by falling
off a wall as he alleges, or did it happen from some other cause, perhaps because he fell
off a horse, or was kicked by it?; (ii) have the plaintiff's parents colluded with a third
party with a view to having him give false evidence on behalf of the plaintiff?; (iii) if
the plaintiff’s skull fracture is found to have been caused by a fall from the wall, was
that caused or contributed to by the negligence and breach of duty of the defendant, its
servants or agents?; (iv) was there any contributory negligence on the part of the
plaintiff's parents for failing to properly supervise him on the day of the accident?
54. If some finding of liability is made against the defendant, the following issues
arise for determination in relation to quantum: (v) what injuries did the plaintiff suffer
as a result of the fall from the wall? In particular, did he suffer a brain injury; and if so,
what effects has that brain injury given rise to to date and what effects will there be for
the plaintiff into the future?; (vi) did the plaintiff return to or commence boxing in 2017;
and, if so, what effect did that have on his brain injury?; (vii) was the plaintiff’s hearing
loss caused or contributed to by the injuries sustained in the accident?; (viii) were the
plaintiff's pre-existing behavioural difficulties and/or ADHD exacerbated by the
injuries sustained in the accident? If they were, what was the extent of that
exacerbation?; (ix) what are the plaintiff's current behavioural and cognitive
difficulties? To what extent, if any, are these difficulties referable to the injuries
sustained in the accident?; (x) what is the future prognosis for the plaintiff in terms of
his symptoms and disability?; (xi) has the plaintiff and/or his parents attempted to
mislead their own medical experts and/or the defendant’s medical experts and/or the
court, with a view to exaggerating the plaintiff’s symptoms and disability?; (xii) what
financial losses and expenses has the plaintiff suffered to date and what losses and
expenses will he suffer into the future, either on an interim or whole life basis?

20
55. It is against that background that the court must decide whether it is appropriate
to direct that there should be a modular trial as sought by the plaintiff.
56. The default position in Irish law is that there should be a unitary trial. In ruling
on this issue the test laid down in the authorities essentially boils down to the following
questions: (1) Are the issues to be tried by way of separate modules readily capable of
determination in isolation from the other issues in dispute between the parties?; (2) Has
a clear saving in terms of court time and costs that the parties might have to bear been
identified as resulting from a modular trial?; (3) Would a modular trial result in any
prejudice to any of the parties?; (4) Is the application seeking a modular trial a device
to suit the moving party, or does it genuinely assist the litigation by being of help to the
resolution of the issues therein?
57. Having considered the circumstances in this case and the arguments of counsel,
I am satisfied that it is not in the interests of justice to direct that there be a modular
trial of this action.
58. I have reached this conclusion for the following reasons: First, I accept the
submission of counsel for the defendant that where there are serious issues in relation
to the credibility of the plaintiff and his witnesses, and in particular of his parents, it is
essential in the interests of fairness that the defendant should have the opportunity at
the trial to test the credibility of the plaintiff and his witnesses to the fullest possible
extent.
59. I am satisfied that were I to direct that the issues concerning liability were to be
tried first, that would put the defendant at a forensic disadvantage, if it were not able to
cross-examine effectively on alleged untruths told by the plaintiff’s parents as part of
the claim to damages.

21
60. Alternatively, if counsel was given the opportunity to cross-examine on all
issues in the course of the liability module, that would entail the defendant calling a
number of its medical and other witnesses twice. They would have to give evidence in
the liability module on what the plaintiff’s parents told them at various examinations.
They would have to return to give evidence in the quantum module as to their opinion
on his past, present and future health difficulties. That would be wasteful of court time
and would add greatly to the length and cost of the action.
61. If the liability module were to be tried first, the plaintiff’s counsel has indicated
that he would only propose calling the five witnesses as to fact and the plaintiff’s
engineer as identified on their witness schedule. That would deprive the defendant of
examining in a proper manner what information, if any, was given to Dr O’Rourke in
2023, about the plaintiff’s boxing activities since 2017, if it is established that such
activities took place.
62. If it transpires that the plaintiff commenced boxing in 2017 and pursued that
sport during the period 2017-2023, but that Dr O’Rourke had been informed that the
plaintiff had not participated in that sport, that would have two serious consequences:
it would show that the plaintiff’s parents were holding back important information from
Dr O’Rourke; and secondly, it would call into question the conclusions expressed by
Dr O’Rourke in his report as to the existence, severity and causation of the brain injury.
The defendant would be prejudiced at the hearing of the action if it could not explore
this credibility issue as part of the liability issue.
63. I hold that where issues of credibility permeate across the entire spectrum of the
issues that arise for determination in a trial, as they do in this case, it is not appropriate
to direct a modular trial.

22
64. I hold that the defendant would be severely prejudiced in the conduct of its
defence at the trial of the action, if the liability issues were to be determined in the
absence of a full examination of the credibility issues which arise in the quantum
module.
65. I hold that even if the defendant could cross-examine on all credibility issues,
both on liability and quantum as part of the liability module, that would involve the
defendant calling some of its witnesses twice, which would do the opposite of leading
to a saving of court time and costs.
66. I am satisfied that in directing a unitary trial the plaintiff will not be prejudiced
in the conduct of his action at the trial thereof. While I accept the submission that the
infant plaintiff is a person of limited financial means and that obliging him to call all
his witnesses as part of a unitary trial, may present considerable difficulty for him; I am
satisfied that were I to direct a modular trial, which would be in ease of the plaintiff
from a financial point of view, it would present a very significant prejudice to the
defendant and may even deprive it of obtaining a fair hearing at the trial of the action.
67. For these reasons, I refuse the reliefs sought at para. 1 of the plaintiff’s notice
of motion dated 22 May 2025.
Should the Assessment of Damages be on a Whole Life or Interim Basis?
68. The second relief sought by the plaintiff in his notice of motion is an order
directing that the assessment of damages in this case should take place on an interim
basis. This means that the assessment of damages at the trial would only consider the
past personal injuries, loss and damage suffered by the plaintiff from the date of the

23
accident to the date of the hearing and in addition, would consider his future pain and
disability and losses and other expenses for a period of five years into the future.
69. The plaintiff asserts that it is reasonable to proceed on this basis as the plaintiff
is currently 15 years of age. It is asserted that the full extent of his disability and
consequential care needs and the costs thereof will not be known until the plaintiff has
attained full age. It was submitted that at that time, the court should be in a position to
fairly assess the level of damages that would be appropriate to deal with his care needs
and other requirements on a whole life basis.
70. In resisting that application, it was submitted on behalf of the defendant that
where the plaintiff has already obtained extensive medical reports and where an
actuary’s report has been obtained which details his anticipated losses and expenses on
a whole life basis, it is appropriate that the plaintiff should proceed with his action as
part of a unitary action and claim damages on a whole life basis. It was further submitted
that the plaintiff should be obliged to furnish full particulars of his claim for special
damages on a whole life basis at this stage, so that the defendant can be aware of the
claim that is being made and can investigate it fully.
71. Whether it is appropriate to direct that damages should be assessed on a whole
life basis or on an interim basis, essentially turns on whether it is possible at the time
that the action comes on for hearing for the plaintiff to be aware of his likely future
level of disability and consequential care requirements. If it is the case that these cannot
be properly ascertained until some date in the future because an accurate prognosis
cannot be given until that time, then it is not appropriate to expect the plaintiff to have
to plead and prove his case on a whole life basis. In those circumstances, it is
appropriate that the court should make an interim assessment of damages and should
revisit the matter when the medical experts are in a position to give an accurate

24
prognosis as to the plaintiff’s future level of disability and in relation to his
consequential care requirements. It is only when that is ascertained, that the actual
financial cost of providing that care and assistance can be properly assessed.
72. In cases involving serious injuries to infants, it is often appropriate for the court
to engage in an interim assessment at the initial stage and defer the final assessment of
damages for some years. This is usually done when the infant plaintiff is reasonably
young, and it is not known at that time whether he or she will reach certain
developmental and educational milestones. It is only when these milestones have been
reached and it is known whether or not the particular plaintiff has achieved the usual
milestones for their age, that an accurate prognosis can be given by the medical experts
as to the level of disability likely to be suffered by the plaintiff for the remainder of his
or her life.
73. In this case the accident happened over ten years ago on 15 June 2015. The
infant plaintiff is now almost 16 years of age. I have reviewed the medical report
furnished by Dr Declan O’Rourke dated 24 September 2023. It sets out the plaintiff’s
current level of disability. It also gives a firm opinion as to the level of disability that
will be suffered by the plaintiff into the future. There is no question in that report of
there being a necessity of waiting until the plaintiff either reaches his age of majority,
or until some further date beyond that, before a definitive prognosis can be given as to
his likely level of disability into the future.
74. In the absence of any such caveat, I hold that the level of the plaintiff’s injury
and ongoing disability, whatever about the issue in relation to its causation, has been
determined to such an extent that it is appropriate that the assessment of damages should
take place on a whole life basis. Accordingly, I refuse to direct that the assessment of

25
damages should be carried out on an interim basis as sought at para. 2 of the plaintiff’s
notice of motion dated 22 May 2025.
75. Having said that, I am not the judge who will have seisin of the trial of the
action. I am merely dealing with an interlocutory application which has to be
determined some months prior to the likely date of the hearing of the action. The trial
judge has an overriding jurisdiction to ensure that the trial is fair. If he/she comes to the
conclusion that it is not possible to properly assess damages on a whole life basis, he or
she in their capacity as trial judge, is always free to revisit this issue.
The Defendant’s Motion Seeking Particulars of Special Damage.
76. Finally, the court must deal with the defendant’s motion seeking an order
directing the plaintiff to provide full and detailed particulars of the plaintiff’s claim for
special damages. This relief is sought pursuant to the defendant’s notice of motion dated
25 November 2024.
77. Having regard to the conclusions reached by the court above in relation to the
refusal to direct that the assessment of damages should take place on an interim basis,
I am satisfied that as damages will be assessed on a whole life basis, the defendant is
entitled to be formally put on notice of what level of damages is claimed by the plaintiff
in this regard.
78. While the defendant has been furnished with a copy of the plaintiff’s actuary’s
report, which sets out the opinion of the actuary as to the capital value of the losses and
care costs that will be incurred by the plaintiff for the remainer of his life, that does not
constitute a formal pleading of the level of special damages that are claimed in this
regard. Accordingly, I will accede to the defendant’s request for an order as per para. 1

26
of its notice of motion dated 25 November 2024. I direct that the plaintiff is to furnish
full particulars of his claim to special damages both to date and into the future within
eight weeks of perfection of the court’s order.
79. The second relief sought by the defendant in its notice of motion is an order
directing the plaintiff to reply to the letter sent by the defendant’s solicitor to the
plaintiff’s solicitor on 24 October 2024. In that letter, the plaintiff was asked to provide
information as to whether the plaintiff’s mother and next friend and father had engaged
with the defendant in relation to the provision of housing to them. The relevant parts of
the letter are in the following terms:
“It appears from the housing needs reports furnished by the plaintiffs that it is
the wish of the plaintiff’s family to reside in the Kildare area. We understand
from our clients that the McInerney family remain on the active social housing
list in Kildare but that the family have not engaged with the council in any way
since 2023. You might please advise whether your clients propose to engage
with Kildare County Council with a view to seeking social housing support in
the Kildare area.”
80. I am satisfied that having regard to the level of damages that are sought in
respect of accommodation needs which vary between €1.3m/€1.4m, it is reasonable that
the plaintiff’s next friend should respond to this query.
81. Accordingly, I direct that the plaintiff through his next friend is to respond to
the defendant’s solicitor’s letter dated 24 October 2024 within eight weeks of perfection
of the court’s order.

27
Proposed Final Orders.
82. Having regard to the findings contained in this judgment, it would appear that
the final orders should provide as follows: (a) Refuse the relief sought by the plaintiff
at paragraph 1 of his notice of motion dated 22 May 2025; (b) Refuse the relief sought
by the plaintiff at paragraph 2 of his notice of motion dated 22 May 2025; (c) In relation
to the defendant’s motion further to its notice of motion dated 25 November 2024, direct
that the plaintiff is to provide full particulars of his claim for special damages on a
whole life basis within eight weeks of perfection of the court’s order; (d) Direct that the
plaintiff is to reply to the letter from the defendant’s solicitor dated 24 October 2024
within eight weeks of perfection of the court’s order.
83. As this judgment is being delivered electronically, the parties shall have two
weeks within which to furnish brief written submissions of not more than 1,000 words
on the terms of the final orders and on costs and on any other matters that may arise.
84. The matter will be listed for mention on 4 March 2026 at 10.30 hours for the
purpose of making final orders.
